risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participating ASUs will be stratified and randomised." "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software." Comment: The study describes a stratified randomisation process carried out by an independent statistician using random number generating software, which is a proper random allocation method and therefore supports low risk of selection bias for sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: “participating ASUs will be stratified and randomised.” “The project officer (SD) will first stratify ASUs according to their category classification (A or B) and then, by referring to absolute numbers of patients recruited at baseline, describe each as a 'high recruiter' or 'low recruiter'. Recruitment numbers will be included as a randomisation strata to maximise the chance of similar sample size in the intervention and control groups. Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software[29]. Allocation will be based on clusters (ASUs) rather than individuals, and the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician.
_evidence.method_summary: ASUs were stratified by hospital category and recruitment level, then randomised within strata by an independent offshore statistician using random number generating software.
context_clues: allocation will be based on clusters (ASUs)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata... Allocation will be based on clusters (ASUs) rather than individuals, and the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician." Comment: This explicitly describes central allocation by an independent offshore statistician and states that the sequence was concealed until assignment, which prevents recruiters from foreseeing the next allocation.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software[29]. Allocation will be based on clusters (ASUs) rather than individuals, and the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “Patients will be blinded to group allocation.” “Both the medical director and NUM of all consenting ASUs will be aware that our study is examining the effect of an intervention...” “medical directors and NUMs from ASUs subsequently randomised to the control group may be able to deduce their group allocation because no workshops are being organised.” “However, all senior clinical members of control group ASUs remain blind to the exact nature of the intervention...” Comment: Participants were blinded, but key personnel delivering the intervention were only partially blinded and control-site leaders could likely deduce allocation. The intervention involved workshops, education sessions, protocols, support and feedback versus an abridged guideline-only control, so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence clinician behaviour and the subjective care-process and patient-reported outcomes assessed in the study.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Patients will be blinded to group allocation.” “Both the medical director and NUM of all consenting ASUs will be aware that our study is examining the effect of an intervention to manage fever, hyperglycaemia and swallowing dysfunction following acute stroke.” “Furthermore, as control ASUs receive a minimum intervention, medical directors and NUMs from ASUs subsequently randomised to the control group may be able to deduce their group allocation because no workshops are being organised. However, all senior clinical members of control group ASUs remain blind to the exact nature of the intervention as described above.”
_evidence.blinding_method: control ASUs receive “a minimum intervention” and “an abridged version of the latest NSF Guidelines for Acute Stroke Management”; intervention ASUs receive workshops, education sessions, protocols, support and feedback; no identical placebo described

temperature readings for the first 72 hours

subjective_outcomes: swallowing screen within the first 24 hours
proportion of patients who meet the applicable clinical care elements

temperature readings for the first 72 hours

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All CATIs will be undertaken by research interviewers blind to the study design and also to ASU group allocation" and "Data entry will be undertaken by the CATI research assistants blind to group allocation." Comment: The main patient outcomes (death/disability, mRS, Barthel Index, SF-36) were collected by blinded telephone interviewers, and the one outcome that was patient self-report was therefore assessed by a blinded assessor. The other outcomes were either objective or were also collected by blinded independent research assistants. Knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have biased outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: All CATIs will be undertaken by research interviewers blind to the study design and also to ASU group allocation [26,28].

outcomes.label: mean finger-prick blood glucose level for first 72 hours
mean temperature readings for the first 72 hours
swallowing screen within the first 24 hours
professional care / clinician compliance from retrospective medical record audit

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The paper is a protocol and does not report observed post-randomization attrition for the analysed outcomes. It states only that "Intention-to-treat analysis will applied" and that sample size calculations assumed "a loss to follow-up of 10%"; however, this is planned attrition, not actual outcome data. No CONSORT-style flow or per-arm analysed numbers/reasons for missing data are reported, so the amount and balance of attrition on the analysed outcomes cannot be determined. Comment: Without observed randomized-vs-analysed numbers at the outcome time point, incomplete outcome data cannot be judged high or low risk.

_evidence.notes: Protocol paper only; no CONSORT-style flow or analysed per-arm outcome numbers are reported. Sample size section assumes a 10% loss to follow-up, but this is not observed attrition.
